nativeRange.habitat: Streams, rivers, lakes, and ponds; often found in clear, well-oxygenated waters including hill streams and lowland rivers

diet.notes: Accepts a wide range of foods. Generally thrives on quality dried flakes or pellets with plant content, supplemented with small live or frozen foods such as bloodworm, Daphnia, and brine shrimp. Has been documented nibbling on filamentous algae (hair algae, thread algae) in planted tanks, which can be a useful secondary benefit.

summary: The Rosy Barb is a coldwater-tolerant schooling fish from South Asia, valued for its hardiness and the striking pink-red coloration males develop at breeding condition. Generally peaceful in groups of six or more, it suits community tanks well.
careNotes: The Rosy Barb (Pethia conchonius) was reclassified from the older genus Puntius — and before that Barbus — into Pethia in 2012; the older names Puntius conchonius and Barbus conchonius are still occasionally encountered in the trade. Several ornamental strains have been developed, including long-fin, neon-pink (sometimes sold as super-red), and golden varieties. One of the species' most notable traits is its tolerance for cooler temperatures: it generally thrives without a heater in temperate indoor rooms where temperatures hover around 18–22°C, and has been kept in outdoor ponds in mild climates. Maximum recorded length is 14 cm, though trade fish commonly reach 7–10 cm, with selectively bred larger individuals sometimes reaching 10–12 cm. Keeping the Rosy Barb in groups of six or more tends to reduce fin-nipping; a group of eight to ten with more females than males is commonly recommended to spread any competition among males. The species has been documented nibbling filamentous algae — including hair algae, staghorn, and thread algae — which can be a useful secondary benefit in planted tanks dealing with algae growth. Long-fin varieties may be more susceptible to fin damage and are best kept away from confirmed fin-nippers.
breedingNotes: The Rosy Barb is an egg-scattering free spawner that provides no parental care, and is generally considered moderately challenging to breed deliberately. Conditioning adults with live or frozen foods such as bloodworm or Daphnia typically encourages spawning behavior. A separate breeding tank fitted with fine-leaved plants or a spawning mop is advisable, as adults will consume eggs if given access to them. Water temperature around 22°C and a slightly shallower water depth are commonly used to trigger spawning. Males color up intensely during conditioning. Newly hatched fry are small and should be started on infusoria or a commercial fry food before graduating to larger offerings.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Primary source used in place of Aquarium Co-Op, whose Rosy Barb page was a retail announcement with no care data. SeriouslyFish provided comprehensive parameters, size, range, and husbandry detail.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Confirmed 29-gallon (110 L) minimum for 6–10 fish, 3–4 inch (7.6–10.2 cm) trade size, coldwater-capable, peaceful temperament, algae-nibbling behavior, and India/Pakistan/Bangladesh native range.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked: scientific name Pethia conchonius confirmed, family Cyprinidae, order Cypriniformes, max size 14 cm TL, temperature 18–22°C, pH 6.0–8.0, hardness 5–19 dH. Native range Afghanistan/Pakistan/India/Nepal/Bangladesh confirmed.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (18–22°C), pH 6–8, gH <10 dGH, lifespan up to 5 years, 30-gallon (114 L) minimum, min group 5. Their stated max size of 6 inches (15.2 cm) appears to be an outlier — FishBase's 14 cm TL and SeriouslyFish's 65–100 mm SL are considered more reliable.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scattering free spawner; no parental care. Conditioning with live/frozen foods and a separate spawning tank recommended. Adults eat eggs if not removed.
